--- SOURCE 1: Shorter Turnaround Time ---
URL: https://www.stratasys.com/contentassets/08bfd36cd4a74fa0a975622e986a9f45/cs_fdm_me_rtm_0317a_web.pdf?v=4a047e

SUMMARY:
With FDM 3D printing technology, the company produces prosthesis molds in a matter of hours and has cut its tooling time by 93%.

FULL CONTENT:
CASE STUDY THE 3D PRINTING SOLUTIONS COMPANY™ Shorter Turnaround Time R T M O R T O P E D I A P E R S O N A L I Z Z ATA P R O S T H E T I C M O L D S A C H I E V E N E W F I T W I T H 3 D P R I N T I N G A WORLDWIDE LEADER IN PROSTHETICS BREAKS MOLD Customized prostheses by their very nature demand high precision, functionality and durability to withstand everyday use. RTM Ortopedia Personalizzata (RTM), a worldwide leader in customized prostheses, based on Budrio, Italy, used chalk molds to cast lower limb and hand prostheses. With the ethos that every patient has different medical and physical requirements, the company ensures that each individual prosthesis meets the user’s need for comfort, durability and aesthetics.
However, the fragility of these chalk molds led to frequent breakages during transit. “The process was unreliable as we could never guarantee that we would receive the mold undamaged,” said Elio Antenucci, CAD Systems Manager at RTM. Additionally, importing heavy molds from as far away as China resulted in expensive shipping costs and lengthy delivery times of around 30 days before reaching the Italian manufacturing house. “By introducing Stratasys 3D printing in-house we can receive a customer scan from anywhere in the world in a matter of minutes and can rapid prototype the mold within a few hours. Since installing the technology, we have cut our prosthetic tool turnaround time by as much as 93%.” – Elio Antenucci, RTM Ortopedia Personalizzata Previous prosthesis molds produced in chalk resulted in expensive shipping costs and were prone to damage during transit.
ISO 9001:2008 Certified © 2017 Stratasys Ltd. All rights reserved. Stratasys, Stratasys signet and PolyJet are trademarks or registered trademarks of Stratasys Ltd. and/or its subsidiaries or affiliates and may be registered in certain jurisdictions. All other trademarks belong to their respective owners. Product specifications subject to change without notice. Printed in the USA. CS_FDM_ME_RTM_0317a STRATASYS.COM HEADQUARTERS 7665 Commerce Way, Eden Prairie, MN 55344 +1 800 801 6491 (US Toll Free) +1 952 937-3000 (Intl) +1 952 937-0070 (Fax) 2 Holtzman St., Science Park, PO Box 2496 Rehovot 76124, Israel +972 74 745 4000 +972 74 745 5000 (Fax) Subtractive Manufacturing a Negative for Prosthetics RTM moved to using 7- axis robots to make leg prostheses from delicate foam, but that required several hours of bathing in resin to harden. This is a laborious process, and in the case of hand prosthetics, the robot needs to carve sections in between the fingers that the drill cannot reach without cutting them off,” said Antenucci. This made the process fall short of the company’s rigorous standards.
After years of enduring expensive mold costs, the company turned to 3D printing for a more advanced, cost-effective and efficient rapid prototyping solution that would also improve the accuracy of the prosthesis molds.
Cut Tooling Turnaround Time RTM turned to Stratasys’ 3D printing technology to produce prototypes with greater and more affordable personalization. This improves patient outcomes, usability and affordability. Additionally, 3D printing is a clean process, enabling manufacturers to go from scan to design to print in a digital-only environment.
“3D printing skips this time-consuming resin-soaking process and eliminates the restrictions of the foam. For us, Stratasys’ technology gives us greater manufacturing freedom than chalk molding and far surpasses those of other artisan prosthetics,” said Antenucci.
3D printing allows RTM to produce individual prosthesis molds by mirroring scans of the unaffected limb. These are then covered in silicon to produce the final prosthesis, with the 3D printed core model later removed. 3D printed in FDM thermoplastics, these prosthesis molds are mechanically strong and perform much like the final product.
The growing demand for personalized prosthetics requires the ability to customize quickly and with reliable results. By using additive manufacturing, RTM can produce durable prosthetics with unparalleled precision in a wide range of advanced 3D printing materials to save both time and costs.
“By introducing Stratasys 3D printing in-house we can receive a customer scan from anywhere in the world in a matter of minutes and can rapid prototype the mold within a few hours. Since installing the technology, we have cut our prosthetic tool turnaround time by as much as 93%,” said Antenucci.
With FDM 3D printing technology, the company produces prosthesis molds in a matter of hours and has cut its tooling time by 93%. Using its Stratasys FDM 3D printer, the company produces a number of prosthesis that surpass those produced on its industrial robots.

# All About 3D Printing Prosthetics

![Picture of Dean McClements](https://images.prismic.io/xometry-marketing/ZqpnZB5LeNNTxq-V_dean-mcclements.jpg?auto=format%2Ccompress&rect=0%2C0%2C800%2C800&w=300&h=300&fit=max)

Learn how the use of 3D printing is changing the field of prosthetics.

![](data:image/svg+xml;charset=utf-8,%3Csvg%20height='1000'%20width='1000'%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20version='1.1'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![Researchers discussing 3D printed prosthetics. Image Credit: Shutterstock.com/Frame Stock Footage]()
![Researchers discussing 3D printed prosthetics. Image Credit: Shutterstock.com/Frame Stock Footage](https://images.prismic.io/xometry-marketing/a49117b0-519f-4065-99db-c93c579e12d4_researchers-discussing-3d-prosthetics.jpg?auto=compress%2Cformat&rect=262%2C0%2C563%2C563&w=486&h=486&fit=max)

Prosthetics 3D printing is the use of 3D printers to design and create artificial, wearable body parts such as legs, hands, and arms. It is a relatively new method compared to the traditional method of production. The materials used in creating 3D printed prosthetics are accessible and lightweight, making the process suitable for producing prosthetics and providing a favorable alternative for patients worldwide.

This article will further discuss 3D printing in prosthetics, how it is used, its history, cost, and benefits.

### What Is 3D Printing in Prosthetics?

Prosthetics 3D printing is the use of additive manufacturing technologies rather than traditional subtractive manufacturing methods to create artificial body parts. These body parts may have been lost through traumatic events or congenital disorders (present at birth). In some cases, amputation surgery may be necessary when a victim's limb or limbs are affected severely by a disease that leads to its atrophy and decomposition (gangrene). In any case, 3D-printed prosthetics serve as a replacement for the missing limbs.

For more information, see our guide on [3D printing](/resources/3d-printing/3d-printing-guide/).

3D printing has proven useful in creating prosthetics for a number of reasons. Using 3D printing to fabricate prosthetics leads to a considerable reduction in the manufacturing time, cost, and weight of prosthetics. Prosthetics produced by traditional methods require stringent procedures and are expensive. A prosthetic arm is usually more than $2,000, and the amputee may need to wait for long periods (between 3â6 weeks) for production and delivery to take place. Prosthetics 3D printing, on the other hand, is affordable, has a short lead time, and the required materials are readily available. A 3D-printed arm can cost $395 and be produced in one day.

### The History of 3D Printed Prosthetics

Scientists and medics made efforts to create 3D-printed prosthetics from 1990 to 1999. There was a breakthrough as their research saw the creation of the first 3D dental implants and organs. However, 3D-printed prosthetic arms, fingers, hands, or legs were still a subject of exploration. Finally, in 2011, a prototype of a 3D-printed prosthetic hand was created.

American artist, Ivan Owen, created the first 3D-printed prosthetic in 2011. A 3D printed prosthetic was not his first creation thoughâit was a mechanical hand. He created this for a steampunk convention and posted a video online of the crafting process. After another collaboration with a carpenter amputee, the project caught the attention of a mother. Her son needed an artificial hand, and prosthetics then were expensive. Owen got to know about her and requested that a 3D printer manufacturing company donate two 3D printers. His request was granted and he created the first 3D-printed prosthetic hand. The issue of the son outgrowing the prosthetic wasn't much of a problem because the printed hand allows for scalability.

# 3D-printed prostheses, orthoses will save time and money — and give patients a better fit

![erep carr mueller portrait 550px](/reporter/images/migration/articles/article-images/erep_carr_mueller_portrait_550px.jpg)Conley Carr, M.D. (left) and Brian Mueller (right) with custom prostheses created in the UAB Orthotics and Prosthetics Lab. When their new 3D printer is installed in the coming months, Carr and Mueller expect to cut production times for prostheses by 60 percent. *Photo: Andrea Mabry | UAB Marketing and Communications*Each year, hundreds of patients visit the Amputee Clinic at [UAB Spain Rehabilitation Center](https://www.uabmedicine.org/locations/spain-rehabilitation-center/) to be fit for prostheses after complications of diabetes, motor vehicle accidents and a wide range of other conditions. A new grant from UAB’s [Health Services Foundation-General Endowment Fund awards program](https://apps.medicine.uab.edu/funding/HSFGEFInfo.asp) is funding the purchase of an advanced 3D printer that will allow those patients to receive their prostheses faster, at lower cost and with an even better fit, says the clinic’s director, [Conley Carr, M.D.](https://providerdirectory.uabmedicine.org/provider/conley-carr/572060?unified=carr&categories=primary_care%2Cclinical_keywords%2Cspecialties%2Cprovider_name&sort=relevance%2Cavailability_density_best&from=search-list) The printer will also produce custom orthoses for UAB patients needing braces after a stroke or other condition, he adds.

![erep carr mueller portrait 550px](/reporter/images/migration/articles/article-images/erep_carr_mueller_portrait_550px.jpg)

This will be the first clinic in Alabama, and one of the few nationwide, to offer 3D-printed sockets for prosthetics, says Carr, an assistant professor in the [Department of Physical Medicine and Rehabilitation](https://www.uab.edu/medicine/physicalmedicine/) and medical director of the department’s Orthotics and Prosthetics Lab. “If you look in the literature, it’s hard to find people doing this kind of work,” he said. But the benefits are so great that he predicts this will change rapidly in the coming years, especially at larger medical centers such as UAB. Investing in this kind of pioneering technology to improve patient care is one of the [main motivators for the HSF-GEF program](/reporter/research/funding-opportunities/item/10191-how-the-hsf-s-general-endowment-fund-awards-help-uab-compete-on-a-national-stage), which has invested [nearly $60 million in internal grants since its founding in 1996](/reporter/research/funding-opportunities/item/10377-health-services-foundation-awards-1-5-million-in-general-endowment-fund-grants-for-2024).

Nationwide, the demand for prostheses is growing fast. According to a [study published in 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590109524000892), the number of people living with limb loss in the United States will double by 2050, largely driven by predicted increases in vascular disease and diabetes.

![erep mueller leg 1000px](/reporter/images/migration/articles/article-images/erep_mueller_leg_1000px.jpg)Brian Mueller adjusts a carbon-fiber prosthesis in the lab. Around 70 percent of the patients in UAB's Amputee Clinic have lost a limb due to vascular/circulatory disease or diabetes. These inter-related diagnoses are closely tied to aging, and the clinic is getting busier as the population gets older. *Photo: Andrea Mabry | UAB Marketing and Communications*

![erep mueller leg 1000px](/reporter/images/migration/articles/article-images/erep_mueller_leg_1000px.jpg)

Those national trends are reflected in the Amputee Clinic’s patient volume, Carr says. Around 70 percent of the clinic’s patients have lost a limb due to vascular/circulatory disease or diabetes, he notes. These inter-related diagnoses are closely tied to aging. “We’re getting busier over time,” Carr said. “We are hiring and growing rapidly.”

Once their new 3D printer is installed in the coming months, Carr and [Brian Mueller](https://providerdirectory.uabmedicine.org/provider/brian-mueller/1978480?unified=Brian%20Mueller&categories=primary_care%2Cclinical_keywords%2Cspecialties%2Cprovider_name&sort=relevance%2Cavailability_density_best&from=search-list), certified prosthetist and clinic director of the Orthotics and Prosthetics Lab, expect to cut production times by nearly 60 percent. Costs will be lower, for both patients and the department, and patients can look forward to even better fit for their prostheses. “With 3D scanning and printing, we can create devices that are tailored to the patient’s unique physiology, minimizing medical complications such as skin breakdown or poor fit,” Mueller said. “The technology also allows for customized features, such as breathability and a mix of flexible and stiff design elements to enhance mobility, safety and comfort.”

![erep 3dscanner printed 1000px](/reporter/images/migration/articles/article-images/erep_3dscanner_printed_1000px.jpg)

## **How prosthetics have been made**

The traditional method for creating a prosthesis starts by making a plaster mold of the patient’s residual limb, Mueller explains. After the plaster hardens, a certified prosthetist such as Mueller would modify that mold by hand. “We remove material in areas where we want a tighter fit and add material where we want the patient to have more space,” Mueller said. The customized mold would then be covered with plastic that has been heated in an oven to create flexibility. Alternatively, a prosthetist might do a hand lamination with strips of resinous material. “It is very labor-intensive,” Mueller said. It is also limited to working hours, and because specialists such as Mueller and his team are in short supply, they are limited in how many prostheses they can create each week.

Several years ago, Carr and Mueller received an HSF-GEF grant to purchase a 3D scanner and carving machine for the Orthotics and Prosthetics Lab. They use the scanner to create a digital file of the patient’s limb, then send it to the carver to transform that file into a shaped piece of foam, which can be cast in fiberglass. “That took us halfway to our goal” of a fully digital operation, Carr said. At the time, 3D printing was so expensive and slow, and the quality was so inferior, that it was not suitable for regular clinic use, Carr says.

## “If you look in the literature, it’s hard to find people doing this kind of work” using 3D printing, Carr said. But the benefits are so great that he predicts this will change rapidly in the coming years, especially at larger medical centers such as UAB.

## **Now we can “go right to plastic”**

Technology improvements in the past few years mean that, with their new grant, Carr and Mueller will complete their original vision. The 3D printer will turn digital files from the 3D scanner directly into a finished prosthesis. “We can create the shape digitally and go right to plastic,” Carr said. Each prosthesis will take several hours to print, but the 3D printer can be left to run overnight or on the weekends, Mueller says.

Another advantage of combining 3D scanning and 3D printing is reproducibility. Although the plastics used for prostheses are extremely durable, they can eventually wear out or be damaged. Having digital files created for each patient means that replacements can be made quickly and can be a perfect replica each time, Carr notes. “We have patients who have used the same socket for years and years,” he said, “and they want the exact same thing. We can pull up the file and reproduce it exactly.”

## Although the plastics used for prostheses are extremely durable, they can eventually wear out or be damaged. Having digital files created for each patient means that replacements can be made quickly and can be a perfect replica each time, Carr notes. “We have patients who have used the same socket for years and years,” he said, “and they want the exact same thing. We can pull up the file and reproduce it exactly.”

## **3D printing for orthoses, too**

Carr and Mueller say the 3D printer will also help create customized braces for patients who need orthoses. “These can come from all over the hospital,” Carr said. “Someone who has had a stroke and has foot drop may need bracing for that foot, while patients from neurosurgery might require a spinal brace.” Not all of these patients will require custom bracing, because off-the-shelf options are available; but Mueller estimates that around a third of patients will receive 3D-printed braces.

The one downside to industrial-scale 3D printing is that it involves a “very large piece of equipment,” Mueller said. The new machine will take up a sizeable portion of the Orthotics and Prosthetics Lab. Carr and Mueller are hoping to expand their space to keep up with demand as other clinical services move from the existing Spain Rehabilitation Center to the new UAB Rehabilitation Pavilion, which is set to open later this year. “We often have more practitioners available than exam rooms,” Carr said. “Additional space will allow us to meet the needs of more patients.”

[Industry Insights](https://www.thomasnet.com/insights/)[Manufacturing & Innovation](https://www.thomasnet.com/insights/browse/manufacturing-innovation/)3D Printed Prosthetics: Manufacturing Process, Materials, and Types

3D Printed Prosthetics: Manufacturing Process, Materials, and Types
===================================================================

Written by[Laura Ross](https://www.thomasnet.com/insights/author/laura-ross) on 6/17/2025. 

12 min read

[](https://www.thomasnet.com/insights/3d-printed-prosthetics/ "Copy link to clipboard")[](https://www.linkedin.com/sharing/share-offsite/?url=https://www.thomasnet.com/insights/3d-printed-prosthetics/ "Share via LinkedIn")[](https://www.facebook.com/sharer/sharer.php?u=https://www.thomasnet.com/insights/3d-printed-prosthetics/ "Share via Facebook")[](mailto:?subject=3D%20Printed%20Prosthetics%3A%20Manufacturing%20Process%2C%20Materials%2C%20and%20Types&body=3D%20Printed%20Prosthetics%3A%20Manufacturing%20Process%2C%20Materials%2C%20and%20Types%0Ahttps%3A%2F%2Fwww.thomasnet.com%2Finsights%2F3d-printed-prosthetics%2F%0A%0A3D%20printing%20in%20prosthetics%20can%20vastly%20improve%20patients%26%238217%3B%20well-being%20and%20transform%20the%20healthcare%20system%20as%20we%20know%20it. "Share via email")[Subscribe](https://www.thomasnet.com/industry-update-newsletter)

![Image 3: 3D Printed Prosthetics: Manufacturing Process, Materials, and Types](https://www.thomasnet.com/insights/_next/image/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fthomasmkt.wpengine.com%2Fwp-content%2Fuploads%2F2023%2F10%2F3d-printed-prosthetic.jpg&w=3840&q=75)

Prosthetics describes the making and fitting of artificial body parts, or prostheses, to restore function or appearance. Artificial limbs, eyeballs, bone plates, teeth, and even heart valves are provided to people who are missing body parts from birth and those who lost or damaged them due to injury, illness, or amputation.

The global limb prosthetics market was valued at[$1.59 billion in 2022](https://www.fortunebusinessinsights.com/limb-prosthetics-market-106893)and is projected to grow from $1.67 billion in 2023 to $2.44 billion by 2030.

3D printing, also known as additive manufacturing, promises to accelerate this growth by offering unique aesthetics, higher durability, lower production costs, personalized design, and operational efficiencies. As a result, more people around the world will get quicker access to high-quality and tailor-made prostheses.

Manufacturing Process of 3D Printed Prosthetics
-----------------------------------------------

The traditional methods of prosthetic production are complex, slow, and expensive. 3D printing offers a more streamlined approach to manufacturing. Below are the steps of 3D printing a prosthetic.

### Step 1: 3D Scanning

Patient data in the form of digital images is collected via 3D medical imaging technologies, including Computerized Tomography (CT) scans, Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) scans, X-rays, and handheld 3D scanners.

### Step 2: Digital Modeling

Digital images are uploaded to Computer Aided Design (CAD) software for prosthetists to design, develop, and modify the required device. The level of sophistication of the design software will determine if this process is partially or fully automated.

### Step 3: 3D Printing

Once finalized, the prosthesis design is sent to 3D printers, which print layer-upon-layer of material to create a bonded, solid object. This technique enables the printing of highly complex structures in a very short period, something that would not be feasible using manual production processes.

### Step 4: Fitting

Healthcare professionals work with the patient to fit the device. Since the product has been designed to perfectly match the patient’s anatomy and physiology, further adjustments should be minimal.

Materials for 3D Printed Prosthetics
------------------------------------

Additive manufacturing materials for prostheses must be strong, flexible, and highly customizable. It’s vital that a finished product matches its user’s anatomy, fits them comfortably, and works effectively.

Thanks to their strength and flexibility, UV-curable (SUV) elastomers are among the most common materials used for 3D-printed prosthetic devices. In 2017, researchers developed the[world’s most stretchable 3D printable elastomer](https://www.materialstoday.com/polymers-soft-materials/news/worlds-most-stretchable-3d-printable-elastomer/), a material that can be stretched by up to 1,100%. Plastics, such as polyethylene, polypropylene, acrylics, and polyurethane, have similar properties and are also commonly used in 3D prosthetic printing.

Exciting technological advancements are seeing 3D printers accommodate a much broader range of materials, including carbon fiber, titanium, chrome alloys, cobalt, living cells, and other biocompatible materials. When used in 3D printing, these materials produce mesh or lattice structures, which promote better osseointegration – the structural and functional connection between living bone and the surface of a load-carrying implant – and reduce rejection rates.

Types of 3D Printed Prosthetics
-------------------------------

The four most common types of prosthetic implants are:

1.**Transradial**: A transradial prosthesis is an artificial arm that attaches below the elbow. It could be active, which means the wearer can control its movement, or passive, which means the device serves only a cosmetic purpose.

2.**Transhumeral**: A transhumeral prosthesis is an artificial arm that attaches below the elbow. This is typically a more challenging device to produce, and proper socket fit is essential.

3.**Transtibial**: Transtibial prostheses are artificial limbs that replace missing or amputated limbs below the knee.

4.**Transfemoral:**Transfemoral prostheses are artificial limbs that replace missing or amputated limbs above the knee. As with transhumeral implants, this type of prosthetic leg is more challenging to manufacture.

Additive manufacturing enables the fast production of each of these medical devices, driving enhanced customization and performance. A rise in bionic prosthetics, in which prostheses are connected to the mind to enable typical human movement, is also being supported by 3D printing.

The technology is also being used to produce less common prostheses, from exoskeletons and jaws to ears and organs.

Advantages of 3D Printed Prosthetics
------------------------------------

Some of the many benefits associated with 3D-printed prosthetics include:

### 1. Cost Efficiency

Prostheses are inaccessible to some patients due to high production costs and the fact that they are not always covered by insurance.

According to the American Orthotics and Prosthetics Association, the average prosthetic implant costs between[$1,500 and $8,000](https://www.prescouter.com/2017/07/3d-printed-prosthetics/#:~:text=3D%20printed%20prosthetics%20are%20created,cracks%20in%20the%20finished%20product). The most sophisticated medical devices, such as myoelectric arms or advanced above-knee prosthetic implants, can cost[tens of thousands of dollars](https://www.medicaldevice-network.com/features/a-custom-fit-3d-printing-technology-turns-to-prosthetics/).

By contrast, a 3D-printed prosthetic — produced quickly and requires far fewer resources — can cost as little as $50. The lower price point of 3D-printed devices is of particular benefit for use with children and adolescents, who can outgrow their prosthetic limbs in months.

### 2. Customization

It is not easy to produce highly customized prostheses via traditional manufacturing methods. Developing hand-crafted custom sockets to fit a residual limb, for example, requires skilled labor, expensive equipment, and a lot of time.

With 3D printing, customization is seamless and efficient. Prosthetic implants are designed to suit the requirements and preferences of the user and are optimized for maximum comfort, usability, and aesthetics.

### 3. Reduced Production Time

It is not uncommon for patients to wait weeks or months to receive a prosthesis, and the process of getting it fitted can be arduous. Patients typically visit a prosthetist between[seven and 18 times](https://pamhealth.com/company/company-updates/life-after-amputation-what-to-expect-for-the-new-amputee), and around 16 temporary pieces are created before an implant is finalized.

3D-printed prostheses, on the other hand, have breakneck development cycles, and first-time prints are often suitable for the end user. The necessary adjustments to improve fit and comfort have a similarly speedy turnaround time.

4. Improved Comfort

3D printing creates highly complex structures with minimal material consumption, which results in the development of much lighter-weight prostheses. Users will be more comfortable and able to wear a device for extended periods.

### 5. Better Patient Outcomes

By automating the scanning and design processes and providing users with customized prostheses, healthcare workers can spend more time working with their patients to ensure optimum comfort and usability.

Disadvantages of 3D Printed Prosthetics
---------------------------------------

The challenges associated with 3D-printed prosthetics include:

### 1. Lower Durability

A professional prosthetist undergoes several years of training before being qualified to design a prosthetic limb.

Because 3D-printed prostheses are often designed by trainees and not always FDA-approved, they tend to break more easily.

### 2. Labor Shortages

Additive manufacturing automates several stages of the prosthetic implant manufacturing process. However, skilled labor is required to operate design software and 3D printers. This is a niche skill set, so the industry might struggle to recruit qualified workers.

### 3. Size Capacity

Some of the most common prosthetic implants, such as transfemoral prostheses, will necessitate large-scale printing. However, many 3D printers cannot print such sizable items and require a device to be printed in several components and later assembled. This slows production time and could reduce product quality if the items are not carefully assembled.

### 4. Design Capacity

Advanced modeling techniques and precise calibration are required to manufacture the most intricate multi-axis designs. Integrating electronics, such as motors and sensors, presents further challenges.

Not all regions and medical care providers have access to such sophisticated technology.

Personalization and Customization
---------------------------------

With 3D printing, the possibilities for prostheses customization are endless. Users can personalize everything from aesthetics to functionality to improve their comfort and well-being.

A sportsperson, for example, will require a highly mobile and reactive artificial limb, while a young child might be more concerned with selecting their preferred colors and styles.

The technology’s ability to produce patient-specific solutions has resulted in some incredible medical breakthroughs. For example, a Belgian[research team](https://newatlas.com/first-3d-printed-lower-jaw-implant/21383/)became the first to implant a fully 3D-printed titanium lower jaw prosthesis. Remarkably, the patient enjoyed unrestricted jaw movement within a day of surgery.

3DBio Therapeutics, a biotech company in Queens, New York, successfully implanted a[3D-printed ear](https://www.nytimes.com/2022/06/02/health/ear-transplant-3d-printer.html), which was made using the patient’s own cells. The implant will continue regenerating cartilage tissue, giving it the look and feel of a natural ear.

Impact on Patients and Communities
----------------------------------

[Research shows](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC9731396/)that amputations of both the upper and lower extremities are connected with psychological problems and different levels of physical impairment. In particular, people who experience lower limb amputation have higher levels of anxiety and depression than the general population.

Prosthetic implants can drastically improve quality of life by restoring movement and independence and boosting the wearer’s self-esteem. For example, a prosthesis could enable users to dress themselves, return to work, or resume a previously-loved physical activity.

Sadly, prostheses are not accessible to all who need them. In fact, according to NGO LIMBS, just[5% of the nearly 40 million amputees](https://www.sculpteo.com/en/3d-learning-hub/applications-of-3d-printing/3d-printed-prosthetics/)in the developing world have access to prosthetic devices or assistance. Cost is the most significant barrier to overcome, but distribution is also challenging, particularly in regions affected by war.

Thankfully, countless organizations worldwide are committed to making prosthetic implants more readily available, and these goals are more achievable thanks to 3D printing.

*   In Kenya, Guillermo Martínez, the founder of[Ayúdame3D](https://ayudame3d.org/)is helping amputees lead a more normal life by producing 3D-printed hands for as little as $50.
*   [LIMBER Prosthetics & Orthotics](https://www.techbriefs.com/component/content/article/tb/pub/features/technology-society/47557), which began as a student-driven research effort at the University of California, San Diego, is focused on 3D printing one-piece, structurally sound prosthetic limbs at a cheaper price point.
*   [Toughware Prosthetics](https://markforged.com/resources/blog/3d-printed-prosthetics), meanwhile, is committed to creating affordable and accessible prosthetics for the underserved amputee community.

Collaboration and Open Source Initiatives
-----------------------------------------

Expanding the use of 3D printing to make prostheses accessible and affordable to all requires collaboration between healthcare providers, governments, local communities, and non-profit organizations.

[The Victoria Hand Project](https://ultimaker.com/learn/changing-lives-in-developing-countries-with-3d-printed-prosthetics/), for example, creates customized prosthetic hands for amputees in developing countries. The organization works closely with local governments and communities to ensure the right people are trained on 3D printer usage. In Nepal, the Victoria Hand’s team coaches local engineering students who have the transferable skills needed to operate 3D printing technology.

Due to the ongoing war, the Ukrainian Ministry of Social Policy[reported](https://www.medicaldevice-network.com/features/a-custom-fit-3d-printing-technology-turns-to-prosthetics/)that as many as 12,000 soldiers had required some form of amputation. These amputees can receive better and faster care thanks to the portability of 3D printers, which enable on-site production and lower production costs. A U.S.-based nonprofit,[Kind Deeds](https://abcnews.go.com/International/wounded-ukrainian-soldiers-prosthetic-legs-us-nonprofit/story?id=98180723), has helped 11 soldiers receive and get fitted with prostheses.

The democratization of prosthetic design is made easier with 3D printing and open-source initiatives, which enable anyone with a 3D printer to customize and produce prosthetic implants.

The[Bionico Hand project](https://bionico.org/about/), for example, has designed an open-source myoelectric prosthetic that can repair itself and is therefore accessible to people with limited financial resources.

[e-NABLE](https://enablingthefuture.org/)is a platform supported by “Digital Humanitarians,” whose prosthesis designs are free and available to anyone with a qualified 3D printer. The platform is committed to making free and affordable prosthetic limbs and has over 40,000 volunteers in over 100 countries.

Ethical and Regulatory Considerations in 3D Printed Prosthetics
---------------------------------------------------------------

In healthcare, empowering patients to make informed decisions is a top priority. Manufacturers of 3D-printed prosthetics must be mindful of this, as well as the ethics surrounding consent and data privacy.

With this in mind, the 3D printing process must be communicated clearly to ensure patients understand how their data will be used and where it will be stored.

In addition, manufacturers must deliver high-quality and long-lasting products. Regulatory compliance will result in the production of safe, durable, and effective prosthetic devices.

Looking further ahead, the industry must address equitable access to pioneering prosthetic devices, large-scale testing for quality and safety, and whether to enhance the capabilities of patients beyond the realm of “normal” human performance.

Transforming Prosthetics with Additive Manufacturing
----------------------------------------------------

3D printing is revolutionizing the prosthetics industry by efficiently producing cost-effective and highly customized prostheses.

In the coming years, the cost of 3D printers will drop, and their capabilities will expand, transforming the lives of many more people around the world. Integrating 3D printing with other technologies promises to further enhance the quality and performance of prosthetic implants.

In 2020, for example, a student at the University of Leeds created a[prosthetic arm](https://www.sculpteo.com/blog/2020/03/25/3d-printed-prosthetic-arm-providing-feedbacks/)with vibrotactile feedback, enabling its users to feel when touching something.

Neural prostheses (NPs) go one step further. These devices are designed to restore the bodily functions lost due to neural damage. Wearers of this kind of prosthetic implant will recover all natural and intuitive control of their body and thus enjoy a better quality of life.

A lot of hype also surrounds advancements in[bioprinting](https://www.thomasnet.com/insights/bioprinting/). Within the next ten years, doctors could be[3D printing organs that patients require](https://edition.cnn.com/2022/06/10/health/3d-printed-organs-bioprinting-life-itself-wellness-scn/index.html)using their own cells, solving organ shortages. Currently, there are 106,800 people on the national organ transplant waiting list in the United States, but just 6,000 organs are provided by living donors each year. This means that 17 people die daily while waiting for an organ transplant.

3D printing in prosthetics can vastly improve patients’ well-being and transform the healthcare system as we know it. To maximize its value, manufacturers must fine-tune the design process and focus on establishing skilled cross-functional teams to aid the development of high-quality, cost-effective, and scalable solutions.

_Image credit: Andy Dean Photography/Shutterstock_

URL: https://endeavor3d.com/how-3d-printing-overcomes-mold-based-prosthetics-and-orthotics/

SUMMARY:
3D printing transforms O&P manufacturing by introducing a streamlined, digital-first workflow. The process begins with a 3D scan of the patient's limb or foot.

enriched_points.expanded_content: 3D printing enables prosthetic production in hours instead of weeks, drastically reducing lead times. For example, RTM cut its tooling time by 93%, showcasing rapid manufacturing capabilities.
With the ability to print on-demand, prosthetic devices can be produced swiftly for urgent cases, ensuring patients receive essential support without long waiting periods.
3D printing allows for easy modifications and customizations of prosthetics, accommodating patients' evolving needs, such as size adjustments or functional enhancements, enhancing user satisfaction.
